narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and a Year 1 school decile ranking of 8—both slightly above average—your law school application stood out in some academic areas, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 52% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass that critical hurdle right away. The key factor driving this decision was your LSAT score of 21, which is notably lower than the typical applicant's score of around 37; this stood out as the strongest influence, pushing the prediction heavily toward expecting a bar failure because lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher outcomes on the exam. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA played a helpful role, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it's close to the average and signals consistent performance. Similarly, your strong Year 1 decile of 8—better than the average of 5.71—also nudged things in a favorable direction, showing early promise in your schooling. Other details, like your lower-middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most applicants, had less sway in this specific decision. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile aligns well on GPA and early grades but falls short on the LSAT, which the model weighs most heavily as a top predictor of bar success. While your Year 3 decile of 5 is right around average, it didn't offset the LSAT's impact enough to tip the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles—but the model is conservative, relying on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the norm, as even a modest improvement could reshape this prediction dramatically. Strengthening your overall academic narrative through additional coursework or testimonials might also help in reapplying. You're clearly capable, and with targeted steps, stronger opportunities await.
